narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put real effort into your application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. The admissions model we use predicted a 72.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application for our law school was unfortunately rejected—it's designed to prioritize students with the strongest projected odds of bar passage success. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which stood out as the biggest factor pushing the model toward expecting a failure, well below the typical student's 5th or 6th decile position. Your LSAT score of 25.50 was the second most influential element, also significantly pulling the prediction in the direction of bar exam struggles compared to the average applicant's much higher score around 37. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA, slightly below the average of 3.21, actually worked in your favor by nudging the model toward anticipating success. Your lower-middle family income bracket and full-time enrollment plans provided smaller boosts toward passing predictions, as did your Year 1 decile of 2nd, though these couldn't outweigh the top concerns. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having strengths in consistency and commitment, but your lower Year 3 performance and LSAT score placed you below the bar for those who historically pass on the first attempt. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male applicant—many in similar circumstances have improved with targeted effort. To move forward, I recommend retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or prep programs, and considering schools with more flexible admissions that value your full-time dedication. You have the foundation to turn this around; reaching out to admissions counselors for feedback or exploring retake strategies could open new doors. Keep pushing—you're capable of rewriting this story.
